name: Showtime Emergency Scenario
description: Scenario-based emergency training: 'It's showtime and the wind picks up...'

scenarios.title: The Wind Emergency
scenarios.description: It's showtime and the wind picks up unexpectedly. Make the right decisions to ensure safety.

weather_forecast.next_2_hours: Wind increasing to 28-35 mph with gusts to 40 mph. 40% chance of light rain.
weather_forecast.storm_cells: Located 15 miles west, moving east at 20 mph

stages.title: Situation Assessment
stages.description: You've just received the weather update. What is your immediate assessment?

decision_point.question: What is your immediate assessment of this situation?

options.text: Conditions are marginal but acceptable. Proceed with the show.
options.outcome: Incorrect. Wind speeds of 22 mph sustained with gusts to 32 mph are approaching dangerous limits, and the forecast shows worsening conditions.
options.feedback: While 22 mph is just below the typical 25 mph threshold, the gusts to 32 mph and deteriorating forecast require more caution.

options.text: Conditions are concerning and require immediate attention. Need to evaluate further.
options.outcome: Correct assessment. You're taking the situation seriously.
options.feedback: Good - you're recognizing that current conditions combined with the forecast create a potentially dangerous situation.

options.text: Conditions require immediate shutdown. Cancel the show.
options.outcome: Premature. While caution is warranted, you need more information before making a final decision.
options.feedback: While safety is paramount, making a cancellation decision without gathering more data may be premature. Check all factors first.

stages.title: Information Gathering
stages.description: You need more information before making a decision.

decision_point.question: What actions should you take immediately? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Contact meteorological services for updated forecast
options.feedback: Correct - Professional meteorological guidance is essential for this decision.

options.text: Check all equipment is properly secured against wind
options.feedback: Correct - Equipment security is critical with increasing winds.

options.text: Redeploy available observers to cover critical sectors
options.feedback: Correct - With one observer down, you need to optimize coverage.

options.text: Brief crew on potential shutdown procedures
options.feedback: Correct - Everyone needs to be prepared for possible shutdown.

options.text: Ignore the promoter pressure and focus on safety
options.feedback: Correct - Safety always takes priority over schedule or pressure.

options.text: Promise the promoter the show will start on time
options.feedback: Incorrect - Never promise a start time when safety is uncertain.

stages.title: Making the Decision
stages.description: You've gathered information. The storm cells are moving faster than forecast and will arrive in 30 minutes.

options.text: Start the show immediately - get it in before the storm hits
options.outcome: Dangerous decision. Starting a show with a storm approaching creates unacceptable risk.
options.feedback: This is extremely dangerous. Starting a show when you know severe weather is approaching puts everyone at risk.

options.text: Delay 30 minutes and reassess after storm passes
options.outcome: Good decision. Delaying allows the storm to pass before making a final determination.
options.feedback: Excellent decision. A delay is the prudent choice when weather is deteriorating. You can assess after conditions stabilize.

options.text: Cancel the show - conditions are too uncertain
options.outcome: Conservative but acceptable decision. Safety is the priority.
options.feedback: While perhaps conservative, this prioritizes safety. Document your decision rationale for future reference.

options.text: Reduce beam power and proceed with shortened show
options.outcome: Risky decision. Reduced power doesn't address the weather hazard.
options.feedback: Reducing power addresses visual interference concerns but not the fundamental weather safety issue.

stages.title: Managing the Delay
stages.description: You've decided to delay. How do you manage this?

decision_point.question: What actions should you take during the delay?

options.text: Communicate clearly with the audience about the delay
options.feedback: Correct - Transparency with the audience maintains trust and safety.

options.text: Secure all equipment against potential wind/rain
options.feedback: Correct - Protect your equipment during the delay.

options.text: Monitor weather continuously for all-clear
options.feedback: Correct - Continuous monitoring is essential.

options.text: Keep crew ready for quick startup if conditions improve
options.feedback: Correct - Be prepared to resume efficiently if conditions allow.

options.text: Let the audience disperse and hope they come back
options.feedback: Incorrect - You should maintain audience presence and communication.

stages.title: Cancellation Procedures
stages.description: You've decided to cancel. Proper procedure is essential.

decision_point.question: What must you do when cancelling the show?

options.text: Document the decision and rationale
options.feedback: Correct - Documentation protects you legally and operationally.

options.text: Notify the promoter and venue management
options.feedback: Correct - Stakeholder notification is essential.

options.text: Begin orderly equipment shutdown and packing
options.feedback: Correct - Secure shutdown protects equipment.

options.text: Brief all personnel on cancellation
options.feedback: Correct - Everyone needs to know the status.

options.text: Blame the promoter for pressuring you
options.feedback: Incorrect - Stay professional. Focus on safety, not blame.

stages.title: Scenario Resolution
stages.description: The storm has passed through. Conditions have improved significantly.

resolution.outcome: After a 45-minute delay, conditions improved to 8 mph winds, clear skies. Show proceeded successfully with all safety measures in place.
key_lessons: Gather professional meteorological input for weather decisions
Document all decision-making for legal protection
Maintain clear communication with all stakeholders
Have contingency plans ready before you need them
debrief_questions: What factors were most important in your decision?
How did you balance safety vs. stakeholder pressure?
What would you do differently next time?

scenarios.title: Aircraft Incursion
scenarios.description: An aircraft is spotted approaching during your show. Respond quickly and correctly.

initial_context.observer_report: Helicopter spotted, 2 miles out, coming from the north

stages.title: Immediate Response
stages.description: Observer reports aircraft. You have seconds to react.

options.text: Wait to see if the aircraft comes closer
options.outcome: Incorrect. Never wait when aircraft are reported.
options.feedback: Waiting is never acceptable. Any aircraft report requires immediate action.

options.text: Activate emergency stop immediately
options.outcome: Correct! Immediate shutdown is the only acceptable response.
options.feedback: Correct. The emergency stop must be activated immediately upon any aircraft report.

options.text: Ask the observer for more details first
options.outcome: Incorrect. Details can wait - shutdown cannot.
options.feedback: Additional details are helpful but secondary to immediate shutdown.

stages.title: Post-Shutdown Actions
stages.description: You've activated the emergency stop. All lasers are off.

options.text: Confirm all lasers are actually off
options.feedback: Correct - Visual verification that all beams are terminated.

options.text: Track the aircraft's position and path
options.feedback: Correct - Observers should track until aircraft clears.

options.text: Contact ATC to report the shutdown
options.feedback: Correct - ATC coordination is important.

options.text: Immediately restart when aircraft passes
options.feedback: Incorrect - Wait for all-clear confirmation from observers.

options.text: Document the incident with timestamps
options.feedback: Correct - Documentation is essential.

stages.title: Show Recovery
stages.description: The aircraft has passed and observers confirm clear airspace.

decision_point.question: When can you resume the show?

options.text: Resume immediately - aircraft is gone
options.feedback: Need all-clear from all observers, not just visual confirmation.

options.text: Wait 5 minutes to be safe
options.feedback: While conservative, you can resume once observers confirm all-clear.

options.text: Resume after all observers confirm all-clear
options.feedback: Correct - All observers must confirm clear airspace before resuming.
